summary: Ancient Greek philosopher, student of Socrates, retroactively called the founder of Cynicism by Diogenes Laertius. Argued that virtue, not pleasure, was the end of existence, declaring 'I would rather go mad than feel pleasure.' His Physicus asserted monotheism. Lectured at the Cynosarges, a temple of Heracles, which may have given Cynicism its name. Diogenes of Sinope was called his 'faithful hound.'

works.name: Founding of Cynicism
works.description: Antisthenes is retroactively called the founder of Cynicism by Diogenes Laertius. He was a student of Socrates who argued that virtue, not pleasure, was the end of existence. His anti-hedonist stance is captured by the quote 'I would rather go mad than feel pleasure.' He advocated askesis (discipline) alongside the later Stoics. Diogenes of Sinope was called his 'faithful hound' because he doggedly pursued Antisthenes. The name 'Cynicism' may derive from a temple of Heracles called the Cynosarges where Antisthenes lectured. Cynics identified with Heracles as exemplifying their ideals. The school encouraged parrhesia (candid or shameless speech). Fictional 'Cynic epistles' were attributed to Antisthenes and other Cynics in the Roman Empire. Antisthenes's philosophical lineage runs from Socrates through Diogenes to Crates of Thebes and eventually to Zeno of Citium, who founded Stoicism.

works.name: Plato's 'Featherless Biped' Incident
works.description: Antisthenes is contextually linked to the famous incident where Diogenes plucked a chicken to refute Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped,' since Antisthenes taught Diogenes. Dog-like behavior of Cynics inspired the school's name. Antisthenes's followers from Maroneia included Metrocles and Hipparchia.

works.name: Debates / Critiques
works.description: Antisthenes held a nominalist position, reportedly stating that he could see a specific horse but not the idea of 'horsehood,' challenging Plato's theory of Forms. In Xenophon's Symposium, Antisthenes cast Xanthippe (Socrates's wife) as 'the hardest to get along with of all the women there are.' Both Antisthenes and Aeschines wrote lost dialogues named for Aspasia of Miletus, the famous hetaira and companion of Pericles. Diogenes was supposedly beaten with a staff by Antisthenes, his future teacher, before being accepted as a student.

comprehensive_summary:
Antisthenes was an ancient Greek philosopher and student of Socrates who is retroactively credited as the founder of Cynicism by the biographer Diogenes Laertius. He argued strongly that virtue, not pleasure, was the ultimate end of existence, famously declaring 'I would rather go mad than feel pleasure.' His work Physicus asserted a form of monotheism, claiming there is only one God who is unknowable by humans.

Diogenes of Sinope was called Antisthenes's 'faithful hound' because he doggedly pursued him, and this dog-like following may have inspired the very name of the Cynic school (from the Greek for 'dog-like'). Another etymology links the name to the Cynosarges, a temple of Heracles where Antisthenes lectured; Heracles was widely regarded as exemplifying Cynic ideals. The school he founded advocated askesis (discipline of mind and body) and parrhesia (candid or shameless speech), practices later adopted by the Stoics. Fictional 'Cynic epistles' attributed to Antisthenes and other Cynics circulated throughout the Roman Empire.

Antisthenes's philosophical lineage connects Socrates to Diogenes and ultimately to the broader Cynic movement, which included Crates of Thebes, Hipparchia of Maroneia, and her brother Metrocles. Through Crates, the lineage extends to Zeno of Citium, the founder of Stoicism. Later Cynics included Peregrinus Proteus and Demonax of Cyprus, while Sallustius of Emesa is considered the school's final member.

Antisthenes also engaged in philosophical disputes: he reportedly held a nominalist position, claiming he could see a specific horse but not the abstract idea of 'horsehood,' challenging Plato's theory of Forms. In Xenophon's Symposium, he cast Xanthippe, Socrates's wife, as 'the hardest to get along with of all the women.' Both Antisthenes and Aeschines of Sphettus wrote lost dialogues named for Aspasia of Miletus, the famous hetaira and companion of Pericles.

Antisthenes appears primarily in bonus parts rather than tossups, typically as the answer to questions about the founding of Cynicism or as context for Diogenes's antics. His main clue identifiers are: student of Socrates, founder of Cynicism, anti-hedonist, teacher of Diogenes, and lecturer at the temple of Heracles.
